verses.flow: The Song of Songs, which is Solomon's.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Shir ha-Shirim" (שִׁיר הַשִּׁירִים) is a superlative construction in Hebrew — "Song of Songs" meaning the greatest or most excellent of all songs, equivalent to "Holy of Holies" or "King of Kings." The attribution to Solomon (לִשְׁלֹמֹה, li-Shlomoh) may indicate authorship, dedication, or association with the Solomonic corpus. STRUCTURE: The book opens without narrative preamble, plunging directly into intimate lyric poetry, a feature noted by the rabbis as evidence of its mystical depth.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth, for your loving (hesed) is better than wine.
verses.note: HEBREW: The sudden shift from third person ("him") to second person ("your") in a single verse is characteristic of Hebrew lyric poetry and reflects the emotional immediacy of longing. The word translated "loving" here is not hesed but דֹּדִים (dodim), meaning "loves" or "caresses," referring to intimate affection. Wine (יַיִן, yayin) is a recurring symbol of joy and covenant celebration throughout the book. TYPOLOGY: The church fathers (Origen, Bernard of Clairvaux) read this verse as the soul's longing for union with Christ, the divine Logos — the "kisses of his mouth" representing the Word incarnate.

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd common plur + suffix

verses.flow: As for fragrance, your oils are good; your name is oil poured out — therefore the young women love you.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Your name is oil poured out" (שֶׁמֶן תּוּרַק שְׁמֶךָ) is a wordplay — "shem" (name) and "shemen" (oil) are nearly identical in sound, suggesting the beloved's very name carries anointing power. TYPOLOGY: The anointed name anticipates the title Messiah (מָשִׁיחַ, Mashiach) — the Anointed One. Early Christian readers saw in this verse a prophecy of the Name above all names (Phil 2:9-11).

words.m: verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix
verb hiphil perfect 3rd masc sing + suffix
conjunction + verb qal cohortative 1st common plur
verb qal perfect 3rd common plur + suffix

verses.flow: Draw me after you; let us run. The king has brought me into his chambers. We will exult and rejoice in you; we will extol your loving (hesed) more than wine; rightly do they love you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The royal imagery of "the king" entering his chambers foreshadows the covenantal union between YHWH and Israel, and typologically, Christ and his bride the church (Rev 19:7-9). The communal "we" suggests the response of the whole community of the beloved, not merely an individual.

verses.flow: I am dark but lovely, O daughters of Jerusalem, like the tents of Kedar, like the curtains of Solomon.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Tents of Kedar" refers to the black goat-hair tents of the nomadic Arab tribes descended from Kedar, son of Ishmael (Gen 25:13). "Curtains of Solomon" likely refers to the rich tapestries of the royal palace. The juxtaposition of dark simplicity with royal splendor frames the Shulamite's self-description. THEOLOGICAL: The tension between dark/lowly and lovely/chosen anticipates the theology of divine election — the beloved is not chosen for her outward status but by the sovereign love of the king.

words.m: verb qal jussive 2nd masc plur + suffix
particle + verb qal perfect 3rd fem sing + suffix
verb qal perfect 3rd common plur + suffix

verses.flow: Do not gaze at me because I am dark, because the sun has looked upon me. The sons of my mother were angry with me; they made me keeper of the vineyards, but my own vineyard I have not kept.

words.m: verb hiphil imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix
particle + verb qal perfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: Tell me, you whom my soul loves, where you pasture your flock, where you make it lie down at noon; for why should I be like one who veils herself beside the flocks of your companions?
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The image of the shepherd and the search for his presence at noon recalls the Good Shepherd of John 10 and the soul's search for Christ in contemplative tradition. The "veiling" may allude to one who wanders without direction, contrasted with those who know the shepherd's location.

words.g: the a collective name for a flock
and + in/with to tend a flock
words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd fem sing

words.g: the + in/with to tend a flock

verses.flow: If you do not know, O most beautiful among women, go out in the footsteps of the flock, and pasture your young goats beside the shepherds' tents.

words.m: verb piel perfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: To a mare among Pharaoh's chariots I compare you, my love.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The comparison to a mare among Pharaoh's chariot horses is a striking ANE compliment. Egyptian war horses were prized above all others in the ancient Near East; a mare released among stallions pulling war chariots would cause chaos and was considered a powerful military tactic. The image conveys the beloved's irresistible, captivating beauty and power.

verses.flow: Your cheeks are lovely with ornaments, your neck with strings of jewels.
We will make for you ornaments of gold, with studs of silver.
While the king was on his couch, my nard gave forth its fragrance.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Nard" (נֵרְדְּ, nerd) is a costly aromatic ointment imported from India, associated with royalty and extravagant devotion. This same ointment appears in the NT when Mary of Bethany anoints Jesus before his burial (John 12:3; Mark 14:3), creating a deliberate typological echo. TYPOLOGY: The beloved's fragrance released in the king's presence foreshadows the worshipper's costly devotion poured out before the Messianic King.

verses.flow: My beloved is to me a sachet of myrrh that lies between my breasts.
My beloved is to me a cluster of henna blossoms in the vineyards of En-gedi.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: En-gedi (עֵין גֶּדִי) is an oasis on the western shore of the Dead Sea, famous in antiquity for its lush vegetation, waterfalls, and aromatic plants — particularly henna and balsam — in stark contrast to the surrounding wilderness. David hid from Saul in the caves of En-gedi (1 Sam 24:1). TYPOLOGICAL: The lush garden in the wilderness anticipates Eden restored, a theme developed fully in chapter 4.

verses.flow: Behold, you are beautiful, my love; behold, you are beautiful; your eyes are doves.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Dove" (יוֹנָה, yonah) in Hebrew poetry symbolizes purity, gentleness, and fidelity. The repeated "behold" (הִנָּךְ, hinnakh) is an emphatic particle of presence and wonder. TYPOLOGY: The dove image recurs in the NT at Christ's baptism (Matt 3:16) and as a symbol of the Holy Spirit. In the allegorical reading, the bride's dove-eyes represent the church's single-minded gaze upon her Lord.

verses.flow: Behold, you are beautiful, my beloved, truly delightful. Our couch is verdant (green).
The beams of our house are cedar; our rafters are pine.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Cedar (אֶרֶז, erez) was the premier building material of the ancient Near East, imported from Lebanon and used in Solomon's Temple (1 Kgs 6:9-18). The "house" imagery with cedar beams and pine rafters evokes both the royal palace and the Temple itself. TYPOLOGY: The cedar house as the lovers' dwelling anticipates the Temple as the dwelling place of YHWH with his people, and ultimately the incarnation — "the Word dwelt (tabernacled) among us" (John 1:14).

verses.flow: I am a rose of Sharon, a lily of the valleys.
verses.note: HEBREW: The term translated "rose" (chavatzelet, Strong's H2261) is debated — it may denote a crocus, narcissus, or meadow saffron rather than the modern rose. "Sharon" (hasharon) refers to the fertile coastal plain of Israel. "Lily" (shoshannah, Strong's H7799) recurs as a key motif throughout the Song. The verse is spoken by the beloved woman, who describes herself with floral imagery suggesting both beauty and accessibility. TYPOLOGY: In allegorical tradition, the "lily of the valleys" was applied to Israel among the nations, and in Christian interpretation to Christ as the humble yet glorious one who descends into human valleys (cf. Matt 11:29).

verses.flow: As a lily among thorns, so is my beloved among the daughters.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: As an apple tree among the trees of the forest, so is my beloved among the sons. In his shadow I delighted and sat down, and his fruit was sweet to my taste.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The apple tree (tappuach, Strong's H8598) providing shade and sweet fruit in the midst of an unfruitful forest is a striking type of the Messiah among fallen humanity — the one uniquely able to provide shelter and nourishment. The image of sitting in his shadow (tzel) and finding his fruit sweet parallels the soul's rest in Christ (Matt 11:28-30). THEOLOGICAL: The delight (chamad) of the beloved in the shade of her chosen one evokes covenantal election — she did not choose any tree of the forest, but one set apart.

words.m: verb hiphil perfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: He brought me to the house of wine, and his banner over me was love ('ahavah).
verses.note: HEBREW: "House of wine" (beit hayyayin) may refer to a banqueting hall or a vineyard itself. The word "banner" (degel, Strong's H1714) is a military standard or ensign, used of tribal divisions in Numbers. The declaration that his banner over her is "love" ('ahavah, Strong's H160) transforms a military image into one of protective, visible proclamation of covenant loyalty. THEOLOGICAL: The public display of love as a banner anticipates covenant faithfulness (hesed) made visible — God's love for Israel proclaimed openly before all nations (cf. Ps 60:4).

words.m: verb piel imperative 2nd masc plur + suffix
verb piel imperative 2nd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: Sustain me with raisin cakes, refresh me with apples, for I am faint with love ('ahavah).

words.m: verb piel imperfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: His left hand is under my head, and his right hand embraces me.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The posture of the beloved's left hand under the head and right hand embracing depicts intimate covenantal protection. In Hebrew thought, the right hand is the hand of power and blessing (cf. Ps 110:1; Gen 48:14). This verse is nearly identical to 8:3, forming a structural inclusio. TYPOLOGY: The embrace is read in Christian allegorical tradition as the soul held securely by the Messiah — simultaneously supported and surrounded, unable to fall (cf. John 10:28-29).

words.g: in/with a doe or female deer

words.m: particle + verb qal imperfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: I adjure you, O daughters of Jerusalem, by the gazelles or by the does of the field: do not stir up or awaken love ('ahavah) until it pleases.
verses.note: HEBREW: The adjuration formula ("I adjure you," hishba'ti) by the gazelles (tzeva'ot) or does of the field ('ayalot hasadeh) is unique and deliberately avoids invoking the divine name — possibly a literary euphemism for YHWH Tzeva'ot (YHWH of Hosts). This refrain recurs at 3:5 and 8:4, forming a structural marker of the Song's major sections. THEOLOGICAL: The warning not to awaken love prematurely points to the sovereignty of love's proper timing — a recurring theme related to divine initiative in covenantal relationship.

words.g: the a mountain or range of hills

verses.flow: The voice of my beloved! Behold, he comes, leaping over the mountains, bounding over the hills.
verses.note: HEBREW: "The voice of my beloved" (qol dodi) is an exclamatory phrase of sudden recognition and joy. The participles "leaping" (medaleq) and "bounding" (meqappetz) convey rapid, eager approach — the beloved comes with urgent delight. TYPOLOGY: The sudden, eager coming of the beloved over mountains and hills is read messianically as the advent of the Messiah, who overcomes every obstacle to reach his bride (cf. Mal 3:1; Luke 1:76-79). The mountains may typologically represent the barriers of sin, death, and time.

words.g: the a stag or male deer

verses.flow: My beloved is like a gazelle or a young stag. Behold, he stands behind our wall, gazing through the windows, peering through the lattice.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Gazelles and young stags were symbols of swiftness, grace, and vitality in ancient Near Eastern love poetry, paralleled in Egyptian and Ugaritic literature. The image of the beloved standing behind the wall and gazing through the lattice conveys both nearness and longing — present but not yet fully revealed. TYPOLOGY: In Christian allegorical reading, this verse depicts the Incarnation — the divine Beloved coming near, visible through the lattice of creation and prophecy, before the full revelation (cf. John 1:14; Heb 1:1-2).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd fem sing

verses.flow: My beloved answered and said to me: "Arise, my beloved, my fair one, and come away.
For behold, the winter is past; the rain is over and gone.
The flowers appear in the earth; the time of singing has come, and the voice of the turtledove is heard in our land.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The seasonal imagery of spring — flowers, singing, and the turtledove (tor) — marks this as a Passover-season poem, consistent with the Song's liturgical use at Passover. The voice of the turtledove (tor) signaling the season of renewal was a well-known agricultural marker in the ancient Levant. TYPOLOGY: The arrival of spring after winter's end is a recurring biblical type of resurrection and new creation (cf. Isa 43:18-19; 2 Cor 5:17). The "time of singing" ('et hazzamir) may also be rendered "time of pruning," heightening the agricultural and covenantal resonance.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd fem sing

verses.flow: The fig tree ripens its figs, and the vines are in blossom; they give forth fragrance. Arise, my beloved, my fair one, and come away.

words.m: verb hiphil imperative 2nd fem sing + suffix
verb hiphil imperative 2nd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: O my dove, in the clefts of the rock, in the crannies of the cliff, let me see your face, let me hear your voice, for your voice is sweet, and your face is lovely.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Dove" (yonah, Strong's H3123) is a term of endearment recurring in the Song (cf. 1:15; 4:1; 5:2; 6:9). The "clefts of the rock" (chaguei hasela') evoke hiddenness and refuge. YHWH's own passing before Moses in the "cleft of the rock" (Exod 33:22) creates an intertextual resonance — the beloved hidden in the rock now called forth to be seen and heard. THEOLOGICAL: The divine desire to see the face and hear the voice of the beloved reflects the covenantal longing of YHWH for Israel's prayer and worship (cf. Ps 27:8; Hos 6:1-3).

verses.flow: Catch for us the foxes, the little foxes that spoil the vineyards, for our vineyards are in blossom.

words.g: the + in/with to tend a flock

verses.flow: My beloved is mine, and I am his; he grazes among the lilies.
verses.note: HEBREW: "My beloved is mine and I am his" (dodi li va'ani lo) is the most celebrated declaration of mutual possession in the Song. The formula is a covenant formula of belonging, paralleling the covenant language "I will be your God and you shall be my people" (Jer 31:33; Lev 26:12). The phrase recurs in varied form at 6:3 and 7:10, each time shifting in emphasis. THEOLOGICAL: The mutual possession of the covenant relationship — not hierarchical domination but reciprocal belonging — is foundational to biblical covenant theology and finds its fullest expression in the New Covenant (cf. Rev 21:3).

words.m: particle + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur

words.g: the a stag or male deer
a mountain or range of hills

verses.flow: Until the day breathes and the shadows flee, turn, my beloved, be like a gazelle or a young stag upon the cleft mountains.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Until the day breathes" ('ad sheyafuach hayyom) is a poetic idiom for the cool of the evening, when the day's wind rises, or possibly the dawn. "Cleft mountains" (harerei vater) is uncertain — "vater" may mean separation or spice mountain, and the LXX renders it as mountains of Baither. TYPOLOGY: The closing call for the beloved to return at the day's end mirrors the soul's longing for the Messiah's return (cf. Rev 22:20, "Come, Lord Jesus"). The cyclical structure of longing, presence, and renewed longing frames the entire chapter as an ongoing dynamic of covenantal desire rather than a static arrival.

words.m: particle + verb qal perfect 3rd fem sing
verb piel perfect 1st common sing + suffix
verb qal perfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: Upon my bed by night I sought him whom my soul loves; I sought him, but I found him not.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Whom my soul loves" renders 'et she'ahavah nafshi (את שאהבה נפשי), where nefesh (נֶפֶשׁ, H5315) denotes the whole inner self — the desiring, longing person — not merely an abstract "soul." The seeking-but-not-finding pattern (biqqashtiv velo metsativ) echoes the lament idiom of Psalms and Lamentations, giving the poem a liturgical resonance. TYPOLOGY: The nocturnal seeking of the beloved anticipates the NT theme of longing for the absent Christ — the church in the "nighttime" of the present age awaiting the Bridegroom's return (Matt 25:1-13; Rev 22:17).

words.m: conjunction + verb poel cohortative 1st common sing

words.g: in/with + in/with a city in the widest sense

words.m: particle + verb qal perfect 3rd fem sing
verb piel perfect 1st common sing + suffix
verb qal perfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: I will arise now and go about in the city, in the streets and in the broad places; I will seek him whom my soul loves. I sought him, but I found him not.

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd common plur + suffix

words.g: in/with + in/with a city in the widest sense

words.m: particle + verb qal perfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: The watchmen who go about in the city found me; I said, "Have you seen him whom my soul loves?"
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Watchmen" (shomerim, שֹׁמְרִים, H8104) patrolling the city streets reflect documented ANE urban security practices attested in ancient Near Eastern city records. Allegorically, rabbinic tradition (Midrash Rabbah) identified these watchmen as prophets or priests mediating access to the divine. TYPOLOGY: In the allegorical reading favored by Origen and Bernard of Clairvaux, the watchmen represent those who guard the community of faith yet cannot themselves satisfy the soul's deepest longing — only the Beloved can.

words.m: particle + verb qal perfect 1st common sing
particle + verb qal perfect 1st common sing
particle + verb qal perfect 3rd fem sing
verb qal perfect 1st common sing + suffix
verb hiphil imperfect 1st common sing + suffix
particle + verb hiphil perfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: Scarcely had I passed from them when I found him whom my soul loves. I held him and would not let him go, until I had brought him into the house of my mother, and into the chamber of her who conceived me.

words.g: in/with a doe or female deer

words.m: particle + verb qal imperfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: I adjure you, O daughters of Jerusalem, by the gazelles or by the does of the field: do not stir up, nor awaken the love, until it pleases.
verses.note: HEBREW: The adjuration formula "I adjure you" (hishba'ti etkhem, השבעתי אתכם) is a solemn oath-charge, repeated as a refrain in 2:7 and 8:4, forming a structural bracket (inclusio) around the central movements of the poem. "Daughters of Jerusalem" (benot yerushalayim) are a recurring chorus-figure who represent the community witnessing the lovers' relationship. THEOLOGICAL: The command not to "stir up or awaken love until it pleases" ('ad shettechpats) implies that divine love operates on its own sovereign timing — a caution against forcing or manipulating what God alone initiates.

verses.flow: Who is this coming up from the wilderness like columns of smoke, perfumed with myrrh and frankincense, with all the fragrant powders of the merchant?
verses.note: HEBREW: "Columns of smoke" (timrot ashan, תִּימְרוֹת עָשָׁן) uses a rare noun (temarah, H8490) suggesting a tall, swaying pillar — evoking the theophanic pillar of cloud in the Exodus (Exod 13:21). The question "Who is this?" (mi zot) introduces a scene of awe and wonder at a royal procession. TYPOLOGY: The ascending figure "from the wilderness" perfumed with costly spices has been read typologically as Christ ascending from his earthly humiliation (the wilderness of incarnation) in resurrection glory, fragrant with the incense of his priestly intercession (Heb 7:25; Rev 8:3-4).

verses.flow: Behold, it is the litter of Solomon! Sixty mighty men are around it, of the mighty men of Israel.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Solomon's "litter" or "palanquin" (mittah, מִטָּה, H4296) — a portable royal couch or sedan — is well attested in ancient Near Eastern royal iconography, including Egyptian and Assyrian reliefs depicting kings borne aloft in procession. The sixty warriors (shishim gibborim) evoke David's mighty men (2 Sam 23), suggesting dynastic continuity and the military splendor surrounding Solomonic kingship. THEOLOGICAL: The number sixty may carry symbolic weight as a fullness of martial strength; the escort "against the terrors of the night" (v.8) frames the royal procession as a triumph over darkness and chaos.

verses.flow: All of them are wielding the sword, trained in war; each man with his sword at his thigh, against the terrors of the night.
King Solomon made for himself a palanquin from the wood of Lebanon.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Palanquin" here uses the term appiryon (אַפִּרְיוֹן, H688), a hapax legomenon (appearing only here in the Hebrew Bible) of uncertain etymology — possibly a loanword from Sanskrit (paryanka, "couch") via Persian, or from Greek phoreion. This linguistic uniqueness marks the object as extraordinary and exotic. HISTORICAL: Lebanon cedar was the premier luxury timber of the ancient Near East, imported at great cost for Solomon's Temple and palace complex (1 Kgs 5:6-10), signifying that the palanquin is of temple-grade, sacred quality.

verses.flow: Its posts he made of silver, its back of gold, its seat of purple; its interior inlaid with love by the daughters of Jerusalem.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd fem plur
particle + verb piel perfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: Go out, O daughters of Zion, and behold King Solomon, with the crown with which his mother crowned him on the day of his wedding, and on the day of the gladness of his heart.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The invitation to "go out, O daughters of Zion" to behold the crowned king on his wedding day frames the entire poem within the framework of royal covenant — marriage as the paradigm of YHWH's covenantal bond with his people (Hos 2:19-20; Eph 5:25-27). TYPOLOGY: The crown placed by Solomon's mother anticipates the coronation of the greater Son of David; the "day of the gladness of his heart" (yom simhat libbo) resonates with the eschatological wedding feast of the Lamb (Rev 19:7-9), when the Bride is finally united with her King in fullness of joy. STRUCTURE: This verse closes the central panel of chapter 3 with a climactic royal scene, pivoting from the intimate night-seeking of vv.1-4 to the public, celebratory triumph of the Beloved-King.

words.g: from in up to or over against

words.m: particle + verb qal perfect 3rd common plur

words.g: from a mountain or range of hills

verses.flow: Behold, you are beautiful, my love; behold, you are beautiful! Your eyes are doves behind your veil. Your hair is like a flock of goats that have streamed down from Mount Gilead.
verses.note: HEBREW: The term translated "captured my heart" (v.9) is anticipated here by the double exclamation "behold, you are beautiful" (Hebrew: hinnakh yafah), where yafah (H3303) conveys radiant, striking beauty. The simile of doves for eyes recalls 1:15, forming a structural inclusio. The comparison of hair to goats streaming down Gilead is an ANE literary convention praising abundance and dark, lustrous quality. TYPOLOGY: Allegorically, the beauty of the bride reflects the church's glory as robed in Christ's righteousness (Eph 5:25-27; Rev 21:2).

words.m: particle + verb qal perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: Your teeth are like a flock of shorn ewes that have come up from the washing, all of which bear twins, and not one among them has lost her young.

words.g: from in up to or over against

verses.flow: Your lips are like a thread of scarlet, and your mouth is lovely. Your cheeks are like halves of a pomegranate behind your veil.
Your neck is like the tower of David, built for an armory; a thousand shields hang upon it, all the bucklers of the mighty warriors.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "tower of David" may refer to a fortified armory or citadel in Jerusalem, adorned with captured weapons as trophies of victory — a common ANE practice attested in Assyrian and Egyptian monuments. The image conveys dignity, strength, and royal honor. THEOLOGICAL: The neck adorned with warriors' shields may typologically anticipate the church arrayed with the full armor of God (Eph 6:11-17), her strength derived not from herself but from her King.

words.g: the + in/with to tend a flock

verses.flow: Your two breasts are like two fawns, twins of a gazelle, that graze among the lilies.

words.m: particle + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd common plur

words.g: a mountain or range of hills

verses.flow: Until the day breathes and the shadows flee, I will go away to the mountain of myrrh and to the hill of frankincense.
You are altogether beautiful, my love; and there is no blemish (mum) in you.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word mum (H3971) means blemish, defect, or moral fault, and is used in Levitical law for disqualifying physical defects in sacrificial animals and priests (Lev 21:17-23; Lev 22:20-21). Its use here is striking. TYPOLOGY: The declaration "there is no blemish in you" foreshadows Christ's presentation of the church "without spot or wrinkle or any such thing" (Eph 5:27), and echoes the unblemished sacrificial requirement, pointing to Christ's own sinless perfection (1 Pet 1:19; Heb 9:14).

verses.flow: Come with me from Lebanon, my bride; come with me from Lebanon. Depart from the peak of Amana, from the peak of Senir and Hermon, from the dens of lions, from the mountains of leopards.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: Mount Hermon was considered in ancient Near Eastern tradition — and in Second Temple Jewish texts such as 1 Enoch 6 — the location where the Watchers (sons of God) descended (cf. Deut 32:8; Gen 6:1-4). The call to "depart from Hermon" may carry a resonance of deliverance from the realm of dark spiritual powers. HISTORICAL: Senir was the Amorite name for Hermon (Deut 3:9); Amana refers to a peak in the Anti-Lebanon range. The mention of lions and leopards underscores the wilderness danger from which the beloved is summoned.

words.m: verb piel perfect 2nd fem sing + suffix
verb piel perfect 2nd fem sing + suffix

words.g: from + in/with the back of the neck

verses.flow: You have captured my heart, my sister, my bride; you have captured my heart with one glance of your eyes, with one jewel of your necklace.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb translated "captured my heart" (libabtini, H3823) is a denominative verb from leb (heart), meaning literally "you have hearted me" or "you have taken my heart." It occurs only here and in v.10 in the Hebrew Bible, making it a hapax legomenon in this verbal form. The term "sister, bride" (achoti kallah) is a double term of intimate endearment used six times in this chapter, combining familial covenant language with spousal union. THEOLOGICAL: This language of mutual captivation reflects the covenantal love between YHWH and His people — a love initiated by divine grace, not human merit (Deut 7:6-8; Hos 2:19-20).

words.g: to be good in the widest sense

verses.flow: How beautiful is your love (dod), my sister, my bride! How much better is your love than wine, and the fragrance of your oils than any spice!
Your lips drip nectar (nofet), my bride; honey and milk are under your tongue; and the fragrance of your garments is like the fragrance of Lebanon.
A garden locked is my sister, my bride, a spring locked, a fountain sealed.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The image of the "locked garden" and "sealed fountain" is universally understood as a metaphor for the bride's sexual purity and exclusivity. In the covenantal-allegorical reading, this reflects the exclusive devotion Israel owed to YHWH alone (Exod 34:14; Jer 2:2), and which the church owes to Christ as her one Lord and husband (2 Cor 11:2). TYPOLOGY: The sealed fountain also evokes the sealed tomb of Christ, out of which poured "living water" for the world (John 7:37-39; 19:34).

verses.flow: Your shoots are an orchard of pomegranates with choice fruits, henna with nard (nerd),
nard and saffron, calamus and cinnamon, with all trees of frankincense, myrrh and aloes, with all the finest spices.
A garden fountain, a well of living water (mayim chayyim), and flowing streams from Lebanon.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase mayim chayyim (living water, H4325 + H2416) denotes freshwater from a flowing spring as opposed to stagnant cistern water, and carries deep covenantal and theological freight throughout Scripture. TYPOLOGY: YHWH identifies Himself as "the fountain of living waters" in Jer 2:13 and 17:13. Jesus applies this exact imagery to Himself in John 4:10-14 and 7:37-38, presenting Himself as the ultimate fulfillment of the garden's promised refreshment. The garden-fountain imagery also recalls Eden (Gen 2:10-14) and anticipates the river of life in the New Jerusalem (Rev 22:1-2).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd fem sing
conjunction + verb qal jussive 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Awake, O north wind, and come, O south wind! Blow upon my garden; let its spices flow. Let my beloved come to his garden, and eat its choice fruits.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The invitation "Let my beloved come to his garden" marks a decisive movement in the poem — the bride offers herself wholly to the bridegroom. The possessive shifts: in v.12 it is "my garden," here "his garden," signifying the total gift of self in covenant union. TYPOLOGY: The call upon the winds to carry the garden's fragrance recalls the Spirit's role in making the bride ready for her King (Rev 22:17; John 3:8). In the allegorical tradition, this verse was read as Israel's or the church's invocation of the divine Presence to come and dwell — an anticipation of the Incarnation and the indwelling of the Holy Spirit.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: I have come into my garden, my sister, my bride; I have gathered my myrrh with my spice. I have eaten my honeycomb with my honey; I have drunk my wine with my milk. Eat, O friends; drink, and be drunk with love (ahavah)!
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The beloved's arrival and invitation — "Eat, O friends; drink" — echoes the language of divine banquet and covenant fellowship. Many interpreters, from Origen to modern scholars, identify the speaker's voice in the final line as a third party (divine or communal), blessing the union. TYPOLOGY: The entry into the garden recalls Eden and its restoration; in the NT, Christ as the bridegroom arrives at the consummation (Rev 19:7-9). The gathering of myrrh and spice parallels priestly/tabernacle imagery of fragrant offerings (Exod 30:23-25).

words.g: to fill or be full of

verses.flow: I was sleeping, but my heart was awake. A sound! My beloved is knocking: "Open to me, my sister, my love, my dove, my perfect one, for my head is drenched with dew, my locks with the drops of the night."
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "my heart was awake" uses the participle form of 'ur (Strong's H5782), suggesting sustained, alert wakefulness even amid sleep — a picture of yearning vigilance. "My perfect one" translates tammati, from tamam (H8535), conveying wholeness and integrity. THEOLOGICAL: The knocking beloved who calls "Open to me" is a central image for mystical union in both Jewish and Christian tradition; cf. Rev 3:20 where Christ stands at the door and knocks.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 1st common sing + suffix
verb piel imperfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: "I have put off my garment; how shall I put it on? I have washed my feet; how shall I defile them?"
My beloved thrust his hand through the opening, and my inner being (me'im) yearned for him.
verses.note: HEBREW: "My inner being yearned" — me'im (H4578) literally refers to the bowels or inward parts, the seat of deep emotion in Hebrew anthropology. The verb translated "yearned" is hamu (H1993), to roar, growl, or be turbulent — a visceral, involuntary stirring of longing. This is not sentimental but deeply somatic language for the ache of divine-human separation.

verses.flow: I arose to open for my beloved, and my hands dripped with myrrh, and my fingers with liquid myrrh, upon the handles of the bolt.

words.m: preposition + verb piel infinitive construct + suffix
verb piel perfect 1st common sing + suffix
verb qal perfect 1st common sing + suffix
verb qal perfect 1st common sing + suffix
verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: I opened for my beloved, but my beloved had turned and gone away. My soul (nephesh) went out when he spoke. I sought him, but I did not find him; I called him, but he did not answer me.
verses.note: HEBREW: "My soul went out when he spoke" — nephesh yatsa'ah is an idiom for near-death from overwhelming emotion or longing (cf. Gen 35:18). THEOLOGICAL: The departure of the beloved and the fruitless seeking mirrors the dark-night-of-the-soul motif in mystical theology. In allegorical reading, this passage maps onto the experience of spiritual desolation following a moment of intimacy with God — a pattern prominent in both Jewish Kabbalah and Christian contemplative writers.

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd common plur + suffix

words.g: in/with + in/with a city in the widest sense

words.m: verb hiphil perfect 3rd common plur + suffix
verb qal perfect 3rd common plur + suffix

verses.flow: The watchmen who go about the city found me; they struck me, they wounded me; the keepers of the walls took away my veil from me.
I adjure you, O daughters of Jerusalem: if you find my beloved, what will you tell him? — that I am faint (holat ahavah) with love.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Faint with love" translates holat ahavah (from chalah, H2470, to be weak or sick). This is the same phrase as 2:5, forming a structural echo. The beloved's sickness is not shame but the overwhelming intensity of longing for the absent one. STRUCTURE: The adjuration formula reappears (cf. 2:7; 3:5), marking a major structural boundary in the poem.

words.m: verb hiphil perfect 2nd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: "What is your beloved more than another beloved, O most beautiful among women? What is your beloved more than another beloved, that you so adjure us?"
My beloved is radiant (tsach) and ruddy (adom), distinguished among ten thousand.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Radiant" (tsach, H6703) suggests dazzling brightness or clarity; "ruddy" (adom, H122) echoes the description of young David (1 Sam 17:42), creating an intertextual link. "Distinguished among ten thousand" — dagul mervavah — uses the military term for a standard-bearer, one lifted above the multitude. TYPOLOGY: The description of the beloved as radiant and ruddy, lifted above ten thousand, anticipates the exalted Christ of Rev 1:12-16 and the transfiguration (Matt 17:2).

verses.flow: His head is the finest gold (ketem paz); his locks are wavy, black as a raven.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Finest gold" uses ketem paz (H3800 + H6337), a double superlative — ketem is a rare poetic word for gold, and paz intensifies it to mean the purest, most refined gold. "Wavy" (taltallim) is a hapax legomenon, unique to this verse, possibly meaning curling or layered like palm fronds. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The description of a divine or exalted figure with golden head and luminous features parallels the divine being ('Son of Man') in Dan 10:5-6 and the glorified Christ of Rev 1:14-15.

verses.flow: His eyes are like doves beside streams of water, bathed in milk, sitting beside a full pool.
His cheeks are like beds of spices, mounds of sweet-smelling herbs. His lips are lilies, dripping with liquid myrrh.

words.g: in/with a valve of a folding door
to fill or be full of

verses.flow: His arms are rods of gold set with jewels of Tarshish. His belly is a work of ivory overlaid with sapphires.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Jewels of Tarshish" likely refers to yellow topaz or chrysolite associated with the distant trading port of Tarshish (possibly Sardinia or Spain). The image of a figure whose body is gold and ivory recalls ANE royal and divine statuary. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The description closely parallels the divine figure of Dan 10:6 — "his body like beryl... his arms and legs like the gleam of burnished bronze" — suggesting the wasf (praise poem) draws on language used for heavenly beings.

verses.flow: His legs are alabaster columns set upon bases of fine gold. His appearance is like Lebanon, choice as the cedars.
His mouth is most sweet (mamtaqim), and he is altogether desirable (machamaddim). This is my beloved and this is my friend, O daughters of Jerusalem.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Most sweet" is mamtaqim (plural of excellence from mathoq, H4966), an intensive plural expressing surpassing sweetness. "Altogether desirable" is machamaddim (plural of chamad, H2530), the same root as the noun chemdah, "the desired one," used messianically in Hag 2:7 ("the desire of all nations"). TYPOLOGY: The closing declaration "This is my beloved and this is my friend" is the climax of the wasf. In Christian typology, the church's confession of Christ as beloved and friend (John 15:15) finds its poetic foreshadowing here. The messianic resonance of machamaddim links the longed-for beloved to the coming King.

words.m: conjunction + verb piel imperfect 1st common plur + suffix

verses.flow: Where has your beloved gone, O most beautiful among women? Where has your beloved turned, that we may seek him with you?
verses.note: HEBREW: The Hebrew verb פָּנָה (panah, H6437) means "to turn" or "to face," implying intentional direction of movement. The daughters of Jerusalem address the beloved as הַיָּפָה בַּנָּשִׁים (hayafah bannashim), "the most beautiful among women," the same epithet used in 1:8 and 5:9, forming a structural refrain throughout the poem. Their willingness to seek him with her (1:4) suggests the beloved's devotion has become compelling to the community.

words.g: to/for + in/with to tend a flock

words.m: conjunction + preposition + verb qal infinitive construct

verses.flow: My beloved has gone down to his garden, to the beds of spices, to graze in the gardens and to gather lilies.

words.g: the + in/with to tend a flock

verses.flow: I am my beloved's and my beloved is mine; he who grazes among the lilies.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The declaration אֲנִי לְדוֹדִי וְדוֹדִי לִי (ani ledodi vedodi li), "I am my beloved's and my beloved is mine," is a classic covenant formula of mutual possession, echoing the covenant language of YHWH and Israel — "I will be your God and you shall be my people" (Jer 7:23). The order here reverses 2:16 ("my beloved is mine and I am his"), suggesting a deepening of self-surrender in the beloved's posture. Paul employs this mutual-indwelling logic in (1 Cor 6:17; Eph 5:25-32).

verses.flow: You are beautiful, O my beloved, as Tirzah, lovely as Jerusalem, awesome as bannered armies.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The pairing of Tirzah and Jerusalem — one the northern, one the southern capital — may carry a reunification typology: the beloved embodies the wholeness of the covenant people. The bridegroom's awe before her beauty mirrors the divine declaration over restored Israel in (Isa 62:4-5), where YHWH rejoices over his bride.

words.m: verb hiphil perfect 3rd common plur + suffix
particle + verb qal perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: Turn away your eyes from me, for they overwhelm me. Your hair is like a flock of goats that descend from Gilead.

words.m: particle + verb qal perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: Your teeth are like a flock of ewes that have come up from the washing, all of them bearing twins, and not one among them has lost her young.

words.g: from in up to or over against

verses.flow: Like a piece of pomegranate are your temples behind your veil.
Sixty queens there are, and eighty concubines, and young women without number.

words.m: preposition + verb qal participle active + suffix
verb qal perfect 3rd common plur + suffix
conjunction + verb piel sequential imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix
conjunction + verb piel sequential imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: My dove, my perfect one, is unique — she is the only one of her mother, pure to her who bore her. The daughters saw her and called her blessed; queens and concubines also, and they praised her.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word תַמָּה (tammah) means "perfect," "complete," or "blameless," from תָּמַם (tamam, H8552), the same root used of Noah (Gen 6:9) and Job (Job 1:1). The beloved is declared "unique" — אַחַת הִיא (achat hi), literally "she is the one" — a superlative of singular election. The term בָּרָה (barah, H1249), translated "pure," carries connotations of being chosen and clear, as refined metal is clear of dross. TYPOLOGY: This language of unique election and purity anticipates the NT description of the church as the singular, spotless Bride of Christ (Eph 5:27; Rev 19:7-8).

verses.flow: Who is this who looks down like the dawn, beautiful as the moon, bright as the sun, awesome as bannered armies?
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The rhetorical question מִי זֹאת (mi zot), "Who is this?" appears at key theophanic moments in the Hebrew Bible (Job 38:2; Isa 63:1), suggesting the beloved's appearance is almost numinous. The cosmological imagery — שַׁחַר (shachar, dawn), לְבָנָה (levanah, moon), חַמָּה (chamah, sun) — places the beloved within the celestial hierarchy. In Second Temple Jewish cosmology, sun, moon, and stars were associated with governing angelic powers (1 En. 72-82; Sir 43), making this a subtle elevation of the bride to quasi-celestial status, foreshadowing the Woman clothed with the sun in (Rev 12:1).

words.g: not to break forth as a bud
words.m: particle + verb qal perfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: I went down to the garden of nut trees, to look at the blossoms of the valley, to see whether the vine had budded, whether the pomegranates were in bloom.

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: Before I was aware, my soul set me among the chariots of my noble people.
verses.note: HEBREW: This verse is among the most difficult in the entire book. The Hebrew נַפְשִׁי שָׂמַתְנִי (nafshi samatni), "my soul set me," is grammatically anomalous — the subject (soul) acts upon the self unexpectedly. The phrase מַרְכְּבוֹת עַמִּי-נָדִיב (markevot ammi-nadiv) is disputed: it can mean "chariots of my noble people," "chariots of Amminadib" (a personal name), or "chariots of the willing/generous people." The LXX reads it as a proper name; many modern interpreters take נָדִיב (nadiv) as an adjective. The verse likely expresses the overwhelming, uncontrolled transport of the soul caught up in love's ecstasy.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 1st common plur

verses.flow: How beautiful are your feet in sandals, O prince's daughter! The curves of your hips are like ornaments, the work of the hands of a craftsman.
verses.note: HEBREW: The address "O prince's daughter" (bat-nadib, H5081) may indicate noble lineage or carry the sense of "daughter of a generous/noble one." The term for "curves" (chammukei, from chamaq) suggests the rounded, turning motion of the hips — a rare Hebrew root. The praise of feet in sandals opens the wasf (Arabic: descriptive poem) with movement upward from foot to head, a reversal of the wasf in chapter 4. TYPOLOGY: In allegorical reading, the church's "beautiful feet" echo (Isa 52:7) and (Rom 10:15), where heralds of good news are praised for their feet — the Bride's beauty is her readiness to proclaim.

verses.flow: Your navel is a rounded bowl — let not mixed wine be lacking! Your belly is a heap of wheat, encircled with lilies.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Navel" (shor, H8270) is debated — some scholars propose it refers to the womb or a more intimate anatomical reference consistent with the fertility imagery that follows. The "heap of wheat encircled with lilies" invokes harvest abundance, a potent image of fruitfulness in the ANE. HISTORICAL: Wheat threshing floors were associated with covenant ceremony and fertility in ancient Israel (cf. Ruth 3; Hos 9:1), giving this agricultural image covenantal overtones.

verses.flow: Your two breasts are like two fawns, twins of a gazelle.
Your neck is like a tower of ivory; your eyes are like pools in Heshbon, by the gate of Bath-rabbim; your nose is like a tower of Lebanon, looking toward Damascus.
Your head crowns you like Carmel, and the flowing hair of your head is like purple — a king is held captive in the tresses.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Carmel" (karmel, H3760) means literally "garden land" or "fruitful field" and doubles as the famous mountain on Israel's northern coast, celebrated for its lush beauty. "Purple" (argaman, H713) was the most costly dye of the ancient world, derived from sea mollusks; it was the color of royalty and priestly vestments (Exod 26:1). The king "held captive in the tresses" (asar, bound/imprisoned) is a striking reversal: the great king is made a willing prisoner by love. TYPOLOGY: Christ, the divine King, is the willing captive of His love for the church — a love that condescended and bound itself to humanity (Phil 2:6-8).

words.g: in/with + in/with a channel or watering-box

verses.flow: How beautiful and how pleasant you are, O loved one, in delights!
This your stature is like a palm tree, and your breasts are like its clusters.
I said, "I will climb the palm tree; I will lay hold of its branches." Let your breasts be now like clusters of the vine, and the fragrance of your breath like apples,

words.m: conjunction + verb qal jussive 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: and your mouth like the best wine — going down smoothly for my beloved, flowing over lips and teeth.
verses.note: HEBREW: The Hebrew at the transition in this verse is grammatically complex — mid-verse the speaker shifts from the Beloved to the Bride: "going down smoothly for my beloved." Many translations obscure this shift. The word "smoothly" (misharim, H4339) means uprightness, evenness, or flowing straightness, often used of just governance (Ps 99:4); here it poetically describes wine flowing without obstruction. STRUCTURE: This mid-verse speaker transition is a mark of the poem's intimacy — voices blend and complete one another, a literary feature unique to this chapter.

words.g: the good in the widest sense

verses.flow: I am my beloved's, and his desire (teshuqah) is toward me.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word "desire" (teshuqah, H8669) is the same rare term used in (Gen 3:16) where God says to the woman, "your desire shall be for your husband," and in (Gen 4:7) regarding sin's desire to master Cain. Here the Bride reclaims the word in its redeemed sense: the Beloved's desire is toward her in love, not domination. THEOLOGICAL: This verse is widely read as the Song's reversal of the curse of Genesis 3. The fallen pattern of desire and conflict between husband and wife is here restored to its Edenic symmetry through covenant love. The Bride's declaration echoes (Song 2:16) and (Song 6:3) but adds the crucial element of his desire — completing a progression across the book toward full mutuality.

verses.flow: Come, my beloved, let us go out to the field; let us lodge in the villages.

words.m: verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Let us rise early to the vineyards; let us see whether the vine has budded, whether the grape blossoms have opened, whether the pomegranates have bloomed — there I will give you my love (dodim).
verses.note: HEBREW: The word "love" here is dodim (H1730), the plural of intimacy and caresses — the same word used in (Song 1:2) "your caresses are better than wine." It is a plural of intensity or fullness. The vineyard imagery throughout the Song (1:6, 2:15, 7:12, 8:11) functions as sustained double entendre — the vineyard as both literal landscape and the body/relationship of the beloved. TYPOLOGY: The vineyard of the Beloved's love recalls Christ as the true Vine (John 15:1) and the eschatological banquet where the fruit of covenant love is finally and fully enjoyed.

words.g: to break forth as a bud

verses.flow: The mandrakes give forth fragrance, and over our doors are all choice fruits — new as well as old — which I have laid up for you, O my beloved.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Mandrakes (duda'im, H1736) were widely regarded in the ancient Near East as powerful aphrodisiacs and fertility aids (cf. Gen 30:14-16, where Rachel trades her marital rights for mandrakes). Their mention here closes a circle with the patriarchal narratives and connects the Song's erotic motifs to the broader biblical story of fruitfulness and covenant. THEOLOGICAL: "New as well as old" (hadashim gam-yeshanim) may be interpreted as the inexhaustible abundance of covenant love — treasures accumulated and preserved for the beloved. In allegorical reading, the church brings to Christ both the riches of the Old Covenant (the Law and Prophets) and the New Covenant fulfillment, all laid up in devotion to her Lord (Matt 13:52).

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix
verb qal imperfect 1st common sing + suffix
verb qal imperfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: Oh that you were like a brother to me, who nursed at the breasts of my mother! If I found you outside, I would kiss you, and none would despise me.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 1st common sing + suffix
verb hiphil imperfect 1st common sing + suffix
verb piel imperfect 3rd fem sing + suffix
verb hiphil imperfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: I would lead you and bring you into the house of my mother — she who used to instruct me. I would give you to drink of spiced wine, of the juice of my pomegranate.

words.m: verb piel imperfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: His left hand is under my head, and his right hand embraces me.

words.m: particle + verb qal imperfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: I adjure you, O daughters of Jerusalem: why would you stir up or awaken love until it pleases?
verses.note: HEBREW: The refrain "do not stir up or awaken love until it pleases" (al-ta'iru ve'al-te'oreru et-ha'ahavah) occurs three times in the Song (2:7, 3:5, 8:4), forming a structural bracket. Uniquely here, the oath formula omits "by the gazelles or the does of the field," possibly indicating the maturing of the love beyond need for natural adjuration. THEOLOGICAL: The restrained, covenant-ordered nature of love is emphasized — love has its proper time and cannot be forced; it echoes the divine ordering of covenantal relationship.

words.m: verb polel perfect 1st common sing + suffix
verb piel perfect 3rd fem sing + suffix
verb qal perfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: Who is this coming up from the wilderness, leaning upon her beloved? Under the apple tree I awakened you; there your mother was in labor with you; there she who bore you was in labor.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The image of "coming up from the wilderness" (olah min-hamidbar) parallels the Exodus motif used earlier in the Song (3:6) and in prophetic literature (Hos 2:14-15; Jer 2:2), where YHWH leads His bride Israel through the wilderness in covenant love. TYPOLOGY: The apple tree awakening recalls the garden imagery of Eden, suggesting restoration of the pre-fall relationship between humanity and God, fulfilled in the new creation inaugurated by the Messiah (Rev 21:2).

words.m: verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

words.g: as/like Hades or the world of the dead

verses.flow: Set me as a seal upon your heart, as a seal upon your arm, for love is strong as death, jealousy (qin'ah) is fierce as Sheol. Its flashes are flashes of fire, the very flame of YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: "The very flame of YHWH" (shalhevet-yah) — the word shalhevet (H7957, flame) is compounded with the divine name yah, the short form of YHWH. This is the only occurrence of the divine name in the entire Song of Solomon, and it is embedded within the description of love's consuming intensity. THEOLOGICAL: This single divine name appearance has led many interpreters, including Origen and the Targum tradition, to read the entire poem as theophanic — the love described is ultimately rooted in the divine character of YHWH Himself. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The flame (shalhevet) shares vocabulary with theophanic fire imagery throughout the Hebrew Bible, including the fire of YHWH's presence at Sinai and the consuming fire of divine holiness (Deut 4:24, Heb 12:29). TYPOLOGY: Paul applies the metaphor of spousal love to Christ and the church explicitly (Eph 5:25-32), and the "strong as death" language anticipates the love that overcomes death in the resurrection of the Messiah (Rom 8:38-39).

words.g: to/for to expire or to extinguish

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: Many waters cannot quench love, neither can rivers drown it. If a man offered for love all the wealth of his house, he would be utterly despised.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The absolute incomparability and unpurchasable nature of love (ahavah) here stands as a theological declaration — love cannot be commodified or bartered. The covenantal hesed of YHWH toward Israel is equally beyond human economy. TYPOLOGY: The NT parallel is the "pearl of great price" (Matt 13:46) and the love of Christ who "gave himself" (Gal 2:20) — not purchased, but freely given at infinite cost.

words.m: particle + verb pual imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: We have a little sister, and she has no breasts. What shall we do for our sister on the day when she is spoken for?
If she is a wall, we will build upon her a battlement of silver; but if she is a door, we will enclose her with planks of cedar.
I am a wall, and my breasts are like towers; then I was in his eyes as one who finds (shalom) peace.
verses.note: HEBREW: "One who finds shalom (peace)" — the word shalom (H7965) here conveys not merely absence of conflict but wholeness, completion, and covenantal well-being. The Beloved declares herself a wall (a posture of chastity and integrity) and receives in return the fullness of relational peace. THEOLOGICAL: The progression from wall to one who finds shalom mirrors the covenant pattern: fidelity leads to blessing and wholeness.

verses.flow: Solomon had a vineyard at Baal-hamon; he let out the vineyard to keepers; each one was to bring for its fruit a thousand pieces of silver.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Baal-hamon" (owner of abundance, or lord of a multitude) is an otherwise unknown location, though the name itself may carry symbolic weight — the great king's vineyard of abundance contrasts with the Beloved's own "vineyard" (her person and relationship). The thousand-piece rental echoes ancient Near Eastern land-lease practices documented in Ugaritic and Akkadian texts.

verses.flow: My vineyard, my very own, is before me; you, O Solomon, may have the thousand, and the keepers of the fruit two hundred.

words.m: verb hiphil imperative 2nd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: O you who dwell in the gardens, with companions listening for your voice — let me hear it.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing

words.g: the a stag or male deer
a mountain or range of hills

verses.flow: Make haste, my beloved, and be like a gazelle or a young stag upon the mountains of spices.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Make haste" (berach, H1272) literally means "flee" or "run swiftly," the same root used of escape and urgent movement. The closing verse mirrors the opening yearning of the poem and ends not with arrival but with longing, a literary openness. TYPOLOGY: The final cry of the Beloved — "make haste, my beloved" — is mirrored in the final prayer of the New Testament: "Come, Lord Jesus" (Rev 22:20, marana tha), reading the Song as the cry of the covenant community for the return of the divine Bridegroom. THEOLOGICAL: The Song ends in longing, not consummation, suggesting the eschatological tension of the covenant age — the Bride awaits her Beloved's return, and love itself is the sustaining force of that hope.
